segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a red-tinted 'FRONTLINE' title card, accompanied by dramatic, suspenseful orchestral music. This transitions to an aerial view of Moscow in September 2022, showcasing St. Basil’s Cathedral and the Kremlin under a clear blue sky. Over this visual, urgent news snippets from male and female voices report on "a major breakthrough for Ukraine," "a stunning retreat by Russia," and "humiliating losses on the battlefields of Kharkiv," suggesting Russia is "struggling badly." A male narrator then states the scene is "At St. George Hall, in the Kremlin, in the midst of a crisis," as news voices continue to mention "Russian losses" sparking "criticism of the Russian leader" and "Russian officials packed the room." The narrator adds that "They knew the war in Ukraine was going badly," with news snippets calling actions "detrimental to Russia's and its citizens' future," and that "Their leader was at a critical juncture." The visual then shifts to a grand, ornate hall filled with a formally dressed audience. Two ceremonial guards in dark blue attire open ornate golden doors, and a man in a dark suit, Vladimir Putin, enters. A Russian male announcer introduces him as "President of the Russian Federation Vladimir Vladimirovich Putin," as the orchestral music swells and the audience erupts in applause. Putin walks down a polished corridor, ascends the stage adorned with Russian flags and the national coat of arms, and takes his place at a podium. The narrator states, "Vladimir Putin would make it clear what his intentions were, and who the enemy really was." Putin begins speaking in Russian, his voice serious, with subtitles appearing: 'Western countries have been saying for centuries that they bring freedom and democracy to other nations. Nothing could be further from the truth.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of the Grand Hall, where formally dressed Audience Members, including a man in a blue military uniform with medals, a woman in a black blazer, and a man with gray hair and glasses, are seen attentively listening as Vladimir Putin, standing at a podium with Russian flags and the coat of arms behind him, delivers a speech in Russian. The scene then transitions to Antony Blinken, identified as Secretary of State, speaking in a dimly lit room with an American flag, stating in English that Putin needs to justify 'the horrific sacrifices that Russians are making on his orders' by broadening the conflict to include the 'United States, with the West, with NATO.' Blinken's voice continues as the visual cuts to Marie Yovanovitch, identified as U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine, 2016–19, speaking in a similar setting. The video returns to Putin, who is shown speaking in Russian with subtitles reading 'The dictates of the U.S. are backed up by crude force' and 'on the law of the fist.' Marie Yovanovitch is then heard speaking again in English, describing the situation as 'a very dangerous moment' for both Ukraine and Russia, and 'a dangerous time,' finding it 'hard to see what the pathway forward is.' A male narrator's voice takes over, describing a 'dramatic escalation,' asserting that 'Putin leaving no doubt his war was against the United States and the West.' The scene shifts back to the Grand Hall, where Putin is joined on stage by three other men in suits, performing a symbolic hand-stacking gesture. The camera zooms in on their hands, then cuts to a close-up of Putin’s face as he shouts 'Russia!' in Russian, amidst the sound of a cheering crowd. The final shot is a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Putin’s face, with multiple overlapping images creating a layered, intense effect, as the crowd continues to cheer loudly.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic black-and-white montage, featuring a close-up of Vladimir Putin on the right, juxtaposed with U.S. Presidents Bill Clinton, Barack Obama, and Joe Biden on the left, each appearing sequentially with a fiery, smoky visual effect. A male narrator states this is "more than he has been waging for years," and describes Putin "confronting five American presidents on a path that has brought Russia and the United States to the brink," accompanied by building dramatic music. On-screen text credits Michael Kirk, Mike Wiser, and Vanessa Fica, and displays the title 'PUTIN AND THE PRESIDENTS' in bold red and white. The scene transitions to a green-tinted, surveillance-style aerial view of Moscow, zooming in on the Kremlin, then pulls back to a global map with a '1987' timestamp, as the narrator explains, "It began during the height of the Cold War." The view then focuses on the Brandenburg Gate in West Berlin, where archival footage shows a large crowd waving American flags and banners. Ronald Reagan, with a distinct voice, is seen and heard delivering a speech at a podium, declaring, "There is such change taking place in the communist world. We must take action to assist the campaign for democracy." The narrator confirms this is "during the presidency of Ronald Reagan," as the crowd chants "Ronald Reagan!" Reagan then famously commands, "Mr. Gorbachev, tear down this wall!", eliciting loud cheers from the crowd and a swell of dramatic music. The video cuts to a grainy black-and-white portrait of a younger Vladimir Putin in a Soviet military uniform with visible insignia, looking serious. A narrator states, "At the time, Vladimir Putin was a young KGB officer stationed in Germany, trained to fight the United States," followed by a female narrator adding, "He wasn't a professional politician. He was, in fact, a professional agent for the KGB."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Fiona Hill, identified as 'Co-author, Mr. Putin,' speaking articulately in a dimly lit indoor setting, wearing a red collared shirt and a silver necklace, gesturing as she explains how one would have learned about the West as an "adversary" or "enemy," and reasons to be "suspicious about the West." The scene then transitions to archival footage of the Berlin Wall at night, where a crane lifts a large concrete segment covered in colorful graffiti, including the word 'Blick,' as a massive crowd gathers below, cheering and waving German flags, with people climbing atop the wall in celebration. A male narrator's voice describes this period, mentioning "Lieutenant Colonel Putin" and how the "collapse of the Soviet Union," spurred on by an American president, was a "defining moment," as thousands celebrated the "end of the Cold War." This is followed by a black-and-white portrait of a younger Vladimir Putin in a Soviet military uniform with a hammer and sickle emblem, his expression serious and unsmiling. Marie Yovanovitch, identified as 'Political officer, U.S. Embassy Moscow, 1993–96,' then speaks in a dimly lit room, wearing a dark blazer, glasses, and a gold necklace, reflecting on what it must have been like as a "young KGB officer" to see "your country disappears" or "is in the process of disappearing," being on the "front lines" as the "Communist Party" loses its "raison d'être," describing it as "devastating" and a "severe blow." The video cuts to President George H.W. Bush seated at a desk in the Oval Office, with American flags behind him, speaking about celebrating "Christmas, this day of peace and hope." The segment concludes with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of an older Vladimir Putin in a dark suit and tie, his stern face illuminated by high-contrast lighting, emphasizing his intense gaze, as the narrator's voice states that "the humiliation at the hands of the US continued."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, his face illuminated by high-contrast lighting that emphasizes his intense gaze and stern expression, wearing a dark suit and tie. A male narrator then introduces "the next American president, George H.W. Bush," as the scene transitions to Bush, seated at a desk in the Oval Office, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking directly to the camera with American flags and a framed photo visible behind him. Bush's own voice is heard declaring, "Eastern Europe is free. The Soviet Union itself is no more. This is a victory for democracy and freedom. It's a victory for the moral force of our values." The narrative then shifts to Julia Ioffe, identified by on-screen text as 'Founding partner, Puck Media,' speaking in a dimly lit indoor setting, wearing a black and beige patterned top with gold hoop earrings. Her voice explains that "Vladimir Putin sees American talk of human rights, democracy, freedom as a cynical fig leaf, something that the US uses to cover itself as it cynically pursues its interests across the globe, as it pushes its agenda across the globe." The video cuts back to a black-and-white portrait of a younger Vladimir Putin, labeled as 'Young Vladimir Putin (KGB),' in a Soviet military uniform with a hammer and sickle emblem, maintaining a serious, unsmiling demeanor, as low, ominous background music begins to play. The final sequence shows a formal event in the opulent Grand Hall, marked by the year '2000' on screen. Vladimir Putin walks down a red carpet, flanked by Ceremonial Guards in dark blue uniforms, as the ominous music swells into a grand, orchestral fanfare. He is greeted by applause from an audience of formally dressed individuals, and a male announcer's voice loudly proclaims in Russian, "Vladimir Vladimirovich Putin!" as he proceeds toward a stage adorned with Russian flags and the national coat of arms. Amidst the continuing grand music and applause, a different American male narrator states, "In Moscow today, an unprecedented peaceful passing of power." Another Russian voice is briefly heard, followed by the initial American narrator explaining, "As he ascended to the presidency, Vladimir Putin was determined to restore Russian strength and empire." A final American male voice concludes, "Russians are looking for a strong hand." The setting is opulent, with white walls, gold accents, and large chandeliers, capturing a moment of political significance.

The scene transitions to an interview with Eugene Robinson from The Washington Post, who, speaking in a dimly lit indoor setting, articulates Putin's perceived destiny, stating he "came to see himself as almost ordained to lead Russia at this moment, and to lead Russia back to greatness," and discussing the lack of limits on means to achieve such an "ordained mission," with a brief Russian voice heard in between. The narrative then shifts to Putin at a podium in a formal setting with blue drapes, placing his hand on a red-bound book, likely during an oath-taking or official address, with the Russian coat of arms visible. This is followed by a digital overlay showing a satellite view of Earth zooming into The Kremlin in Moscow, Russia, with green targeting graphics and signal status indicators, suggesting surveillance or geopolitical focus, while a male voiceover announces, "President Clinton arrived in Moscow carrying a message of cooperation." The scene cuts to a meeting between Putin and Bill Clinton in an ornate room with golden chairs and flags of Russia and the United States, where they shake hands and sit for a discussion, with Clinton gesturing as he speaks. The voiceover continues, detailing that "Putin carried his simmering grievance with the United States into his first meeting with President Bill Clinton," and elaborating on their "first presidential encounter" at the Kremlin, noting Clinton's desire to "get a little bit of a feel" and meet him "as president." segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Jake Sullivan, identified as 'State Dept., 2008–13', speaking in a dimly lit, formal setting. His voice describes how Vladimir Putin, seen in subsequent archival footage with Bill Clinton, uses body language—spreading his legs, slouching in his chair, and giving dismissive hand waves—to project an 'alpha male' image. This commentary plays over archival footage of Bill Clinton, a man with graying hair, and Vladimir Putin, a man with a receding hairline, seated across from each other in an ornate room with gilded chairs and patterned drapery, flanked by U.S. and Russian flags during a diplomatic meeting, with Putin appearing attentive and composed. Peter Baker, co-author of 'Kremlin Rising,' then appears speaking in a similar formal setting, his voice continuing the analysis, stating that Putin was a 'cold fish' in his first meeting with Clinton, showing no interest, which left Clinton feeling 'insulted and rebuffed.' A distinct narrator's voice then adds that if Clinton hoped for a foreign policy triumph, he wouldn't get it there. The scene transitions to archival footage of a black limousine, likely Clinton’s, arriving, followed by Clinton entering a building and being warmly embraced by another man in a dark suit. The narrator explains that later that day, Clinton received a warmer reception from Putin's mentor, former President Boris Yeltsin, who issued a warning about the new Russian president. The segment concludes with a black-and-white archival image of Bill Clinton shaking hands with Boris Yeltsin, an older man with white hair, in a formal room with a bookshelf, as the narrator quotes Yeltsin expressing concern to Clinton about 'this young man' (Putin) to whom the presidency was turned over, noting he 'doesn't have democracy.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Strobe Talbott, identified by on-screen text as Deputy Secretary of State from 1994–2001, speaking in a dimly lit, formal indoor setting. He is bald, wearing a dark suit over a light blue and white striped shirt, and gestures with his right hand as he narrates, describing an event where someone 'poked him in his heart.' The scene transitions to black-and-white archival footage showing Boris Yeltsin, an older man with white hair in a dark suit, seated across from Bill Clinton, who is also in a dark suit and tie, shaking hands in a formal room with a bookshelf. Talbott's narration continues, recalling Yeltsin's expression and stating, 'I think it really shook Yeltsin a bit.' A crowd chant of 'Vladimir Vladimirovich!' is heard as the video shifts to color footage of Bill Clinton and Vladimir Putin walking side-by-side through an ornate, golden-hued hall with red carpeting and ceremonial chairs. Clinton, with graying hair and a pink tie, and Putin, with a receding hairline and a patterned tie, walk with composed expressions, while a male narrator explains that 'Clinton walked a careful line, not revealing his concerns about Putin.' The scene cuts to Clinton seated at a table with microphones, speaking earnestly, with an American flag visible behind him. His voice is heard, stating his 'personal assessment' that Putin is 'fully capable of building a prosperous, strong Russia while preserving freedom and pluralism and the rule of law.' The camera then focuses on Putin, wearing headphones, listening intently with a serious expression, as Clinton concludes, 'It's a big challenge. I think he's fully capable of doing it.' The video alternates between close-ups of Clinton speaking and Putin listening, emphasizing the diplomatic exchange. The male narrator returns, stating, 'But from the start, Clinton's private fears about Putin were being realized.' The final shot shows a large outdoor crowd seated in rows, including formally dressed audience members such as a man in a blue military uniform with medals and a woman in a black blazer with a white blouse, all attentively watching the proceedings, with a red-brick fortress (the Kremlin) visible in the background, accompanied by a loud, cheering crowd and a dramatic musical swell.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of a military parade in Red Square, Moscow, under a clear blue sky, with the Kremlin and St. Basil’s Cathedral visible in the background, as a red flag with a hammer and sickle waves prominently and rows of uniformed soldiers march in formation to the sound of a patriotic Russian orchestral anthem with a choir. The camera cuts to a close-up of Vladimir Putin, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, standing solemnly among military officials and dignitaries, while a male commentator states that "from the very beginning you saw in Putin somebody who didn't have the values of the West." The commentator continues, describing how Putin is "dismantling the structures of democracy" and "reconsolidating control in the Kremlin," asserting he is "not a Westernizer" but an "old-fashioned Russian autocrat." The scene transitions to a wide aerial view of the Kremlin, overlaid with a green digital targeting interface labeled 'THE KREMLIN, MOSCOW, RUSSIA' and 'SIGNAL STATUS,' suggesting surveillance, before shifting to a similar satellite view interface over Washington, D.C. Another male commentator then discusses "one of the closest elections in American history" and the "start of a new presidency in the US," mentioning that votes would go to "George W. Bush, the governor of Texas." The commentator adds that "the winner had expressed optimism about being able to get along with Putin." Finally, George W. Bush, dressed in a dark suit with a blue patterned tie, is seen speaking at a podium flanked by American flags, gesturing with a serious expression, as he himself states, "I look forward to working with Mr. Putin and explaining my point of view and my attitude about the post-Cold War era."

This transitions to George W. Bush, seen speaking at a podium flanked by American flags, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, delivering a speech with a serious demeanor. His voice is heard declaring, "you're no longer the enemy... we're not your enemy, surely we can work together. We're a peaceful nation," expressing a desire to work with Russia. The scene then shifts to a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, stern-faced in a dark suit, looking intently off-screen. Heather Conley, identified by on-screen text as 'Dep. Asst. Her voice explains that "Putin saw in President Bush someone that he could do business with," seeking "a new great power relationship" where Bush could be a leader to "redefine the international system." Archival black-and-white footage shows George W. Bush and John Bolton, both in suits, listening intently with an American flag in the background. John Bolton, identified as 'Undersecretary of State, 2001–05', then speaks in a color interview, wearing a dark suit, light blue shirt, and red-striped tie. His voice comments on "Putin's early moves consolidating power" and how his past "as a KGB agent didn't deter the Bush team." He elaborates that it didn't bother him, as "in the KGB you were taught to think for yourself," viewing it as an "opportunity" rather than looking through "rose-colored glasses," seeing "real opportunities to work together." A male narrator's voice states, "President George Bush has called for a new approach," followed by another narrator adding, "The two presidents would size each other up." The segment concludes with a digital satellite interface zooming from a global view to 'THE WHITE HOUSE' and then to 'BRDO CASTLE, LJUBLJANA, SLOVENIA', marked with green targeting graphics and signal status indicators, as George W. Bush's voice is heard again, mentioning "Russian President Vladimir Putin."

Bush and Laura Bush are then seen exiting the aircraft, with Bush waving to the crowd, as a female commentator discusses the development of a "personal relationship" between the President and Putin. A male voice with a Russian accent briefly mentions "George Bush" over the continuing music. The scene transitions to a black-and-white sequence, showing George W. Bush and Vladimir Putin walking side-by-side in a wooded area, engaged in conversation, with close-ups highlighting Putin’s intense, focused expression. During this, a female voice describes Putin's efforts to find Bush's weaknesses and appeal to his personality, while another male voice notes Putin's focus on Bush's "strong Christian faith." John Beyrle, identified as U.S. Ambassador to Russia (2008–12), then appears in an interview, speaking in a calm, informative tone about how President Putin shared a personal story with Bush regarding his dacha burning down and the loss and recovery of a religious medallion belonging to his mother, an "affecting emotional story" that had an impact on Bush. The video returns to color, showing Bush and Putin at a joint outdoor press conference with American and Russian flags and a 'Bush Putin Slovenia Summit 2001' sign visible. The sound of camera clicks precedes a reporter's question to President Bush, asking if Putin is "a man that Americans can trust." Putin is seen speaking at the podium with a serious expression while Bush listens attentively, before Bush responds directly, stating, "I'll answer the question. I looked the man in the eye. I found him to be very straightforward."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white shot of Vladimir Putin, a man with a receding hairline, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, smiling alongside another man, as a male voice declares, "the leader of the West is the enemy number one." Ominous, suspenseful music with electronic sounds and a ticking rhythm builds as the scene transitions to a green-tinted satellite interface. This interface zooms from Earth to Brdo Castle in Ljubljana, Slovenia, marked with targeting graphics and a 'SIGNAL STATUS' bar, then shifts to Baghdad, Iraq, with similar targeting, while a narrator announces, "The countdown to war, it appears, is now underway." The view cuts to nighttime footage of explosions and fires erupting across Baghdad, overlaid with 'MARCH 2003', marking the start of the Iraq War. Multiple narrators describe this as "more fuel for Putin's grievance with the US," a "US-led attack on Iraq," "America at war," and "Tonight, shock and awe." The dramatic music intensifies as a large bronze statue of Saddam Hussein is shown being defaced and toppled by U.S. soldiers, one placing an American flag over its head, amidst chaotic scenes and reacting crowds. Voices declare this "regime change at the hands of the Bush administration," with "The stars and stripes have come to town," and "Saddam Hussein's reign of terror is over." A female voice explains that "The Iraq War was absolutely a turning point in a very significant moment of rupture in the relationship between Putin and President Bush." The segment concludes with a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, seated at a podium, hands clasped over his mouth, appearing contemplative or concerned, as a male voice states, "For Putin, echoes of the final days of the Soviet Union," and another proclaims, "The tyrant has fallen and Iraq is free," adding, "And everywhere that freedom stirs, let tyrants fear," before concluding with "An adventure like the Iraq War."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Eugene Robinson, a man with glasses and a mustache, wearing a dark blazer over a blue shirt, speaking in a dimly lit indoor setting. His serious voice describes a scenario where 'they'll just come in and they'll just take out a government and think nothing of it,' emphasizing that 'that's not the way he wanted the world to work,' all set against a subtle, dramatic background score. The scene then transitions to a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, contemplative with hands clasped, under dramatic lighting, before cutting to archival footage of George W. Bush, wearing a dark suit and blue patterned tie, delivering a speech at a podium with an American flag in the background. Bush's distinct voice declares, 'And it is both our responsibility and our privilege to fight freedom's fight,' which is met with a wide shot of a crowded legislative chamber erupting in applause. The video returns to a close-up of Bush with an intense expression, then back to a high-contrast, black-and-white shot of Putin, looking downward with a stern, focused gaze. A narrator's voice explains that 'Bush called it the freedom agenda,' a plan to 'export American democracy around the world,' and that 'Putin saw it as a direct threat to his own plan to restore Russian greatness.' Evan Osnos, identified by on-screen text as from The New Yorker, then appears in a dimly lit setting, wearing a gray suit and light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera. His clear voice elaborates that 'Vladimir Putin concluded that the United States, when possible, would use its power and leverage to depose leaders that it did not agree with,' viewing it as 'an existential threat.' The final sequence features a digital satellite interface, first showing an aerial view of Baghdad, Iraq, with green targeting graphics and a 'SIGNAL STATUS' bar, then zooming out to a global map highlighting Russia, Ukraine, Georgia, and Kyrgyzstan in green. Over these strategic visuals, the narrator's voice returns, stating that 'the Bush doctrine is spreading freedom around the world,' and that 'Under Bush, that threat was extending to Putin's own backyard,' referencing 'popular uprisings in former Soviet Republics,' as the dramatic background music intensifies with subtle electronic sounds.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a large, energetic crowd marching in a city square, waving Ukrainian and orange flags, as an unidentified male voice states that democracy can be patterned almost anywhere. This transitions to a chaotic legislative chamber with people scrambling and shouting, while a second male voice declares that Americans respect their courageous choice for liberty and will stand with them. The footage then cuts to Stephen Hadley, National Security Adviser (2005–09), speaking seriously in a dimly lit room, explaining that Putin concluded the US and its intelligence services were installing anti-Russian regimes in neighboring countries. This is intercut with a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, his expression intense and focused. An unidentified male voice then states that "Putin had seen enough, he would fight back," as the narrative transitions to a digital satellite interface, zooming from a global view to The Kremlin in Moscow and then Munich, Germany, with targeting graphics, accompanied by a camera shutter sound. An unidentified female news reporter announces that Russian President Vladimir Putin is speaking at an international conference in Germany, as a motorcade with a black Mercedes-Benz and police escort is seen in Munich. Another unidentified male voice confirms Putin would do it in Munich, issuing a warning to America and the world, followed by a brief comment in German about Putin's harsh words from an unidentified German male reporter. Masha Gessen, author of 'The Man Without a Face,' then speaks with urgency, describing Putin at the Munich security conference, not mincing words, and listing all his resentments. The final shot shows an unidentified man in a suit and tie speaking at a podium with 'Friendship' in the background, beginning with "First and foremost."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Vladimir Putin, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, delivering a serious speech in Russian at the Munich Conference on Security Policy in 2007, as on-screen text confirms. He asserts that the United States has "overstepped its national borders" in economic, political, and humanitarian spheres, imposing its will on other states, and questions, "Who would like this?" The camera cuts to the formally dressed audience, including Strobe Talbott, listening intently. A wide shot reveals the conference hall with banners reading 'Münchner Konferenz für Sicherheitspolitik, 9–11. Februar 2007' and 'Frieden durch Dialog'. As Putin continues to speak in Russian, emphasizing that this situation is "extremely dangerous" and that "no one feels safe anymore," Strobe Talbott, identified as Deputy Secretary of State, 1994–2001, provides a reflective English voiceover, describing Putin's speech as "so searing and blunt," stating, "this was the real guy." The scene then transitions to black-and-white footage of Putin walking through photographers, followed by an interview with Daniel Fried, Assistant Secretary of State, 2005–09, who comments on Putin "accusing the US of making the world unsafe." Finally, Strobe Talbott, in a dimly lit interview setting, further analyzes Putin's address, stating he "was drawing a line and saying, 'We're not going to try anymore. We're just giving up on you, and we're going to make our own world.'" The atmosphere remains formal and tense, reflecting the gravity of the geopolitical discourse.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a serious musical underscore as a narrator introduces the topic of Georgia and Ukraine's potential NATO membership. Visually, George W. Bush is seen waving as he exits a vehicle, flanked by Ceremonial Guards in dark blue uniforms with gold trim, opening ornate golden doors. John Beyrle, U.S. Ambassador to Russia (2008–12), then speaks in an interview, his clear, measured voice expressing Russia's profound concern that the Bush administration was moving towards allowing both Georgia and Ukraine into NATO, which he describes as a 'huge red line.' The scene transitions to Vladimir Putin exiting a black limousine on a red carpet, accompanied by security, followed by black-and-white archival images of him in formal settings, including a close-up of his intense gaze. The narrator's authoritative voice explains that Putin rushed to Bucharest to confront Bush. William Taylor, U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine (2006–09), then speaks in an interview, his distinct, slightly gravelly voice recounting an 'amazing moment' where Putin 'shows up early and crashes the dinner' with NATO members, effectively acting as 'the skunk in the garden' and 'challenging them for what they've done.' The narrator continues, stating that in a later meeting, Putin was adamant to Bush that Georgia and especially Ukraine were in 'his sphere of influence.' Taylor then quotes Putin as having leaned over and said, 'Ukraine is not a real country,' emphasizing that Putin had 'no respect for the sovereignty of the Ukrainian people,' concluding with a reflective thought that 'we should have seen it earlier.' The overall tone remains serious and reflective, focusing on high-stakes diplomatic interactions.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Steven Pifer, a man in a dark suit and tie, speaking earnestly in a dimly lit room, stating, "Let's clear if we wanted to see it, the direction that President Putin was going." The scene transitions to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, shown with a serious, contemplative expression, his face partially obscured, as a male narrator declares, "Putin made it clear he wouldn't accept Bush's plan for Georgia and Ukraine." Julia Ioffe, identified as 'Founding partner, Puck Media', then appears, a woman with shoulder-length brown hair and gold hoop earrings, speaking in a dimly lit setting. She explains, "It basically waved the red flag in front of the bull. It was extremely provocative for Russia. It provoked Russia and made Russia angry." The video cuts to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Putin’s intense eyes, while the narrator continues, "Just four months later, Putin attacked Georgia." This is followed by chaotic footage of military tanks moving through a war-torn urban environment with buildings ablaze and smoke rising, accompanied by the urgent voice of a news reporter describing "Russian troops backed by tanks and armored vehicles..." Steven Pifer, now identified as U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine (1998–2000), speaks again with a serious expression, stating, "The Russians were prepared and perhaps even eager for a conflict with Georgia. There appeared to be little to stop the troops in their advance," and continuing over a shaky, handheld shot of a man running through rubble, "And part of that was motivated by we're going to show the Georgians that getting too close to NATO has a price." The human cost of war is highlighted by a scene of a person lying injured on the ground amidst debris, as an urgent news report mentions "Russian planes again bombing Georgian..." The final frames depict a soldier in camouflage moving cautiously through a destroyed building, reinforcing the ongoing conflict and destruction, while the narrator adds, "Putin seized and held nearly 5,000 square miles of Georgian territory." An urgent news report further details, "...another town is burning, more civilians have been killed," and another male voice offers analysis, "From Putin's point of view, this wasn't just about Georgia," amidst sounds of conflict.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white close-up of George W. Bush, his expression serious and contemplative, as ominous, low-frequency background music begins. Security Council, 2004–07', who speaks in a clear, measured voice from a dimly lit room, stating, "I remember the president saying, you know, I don't know how, but we've lost him." He continues, explaining that "Putin was going in a different direction, and there was little that the administration, uh, in President Bush's mind could do to put Putin back on that course." A male narrator then adds, "Nearing the end of his presidency, Bush would leave the increasing conflict with Putin to his successor." The video cuts back to a series of dramatic black-and-white close-ups: first, George W. Bush with a furrowed brow, then Vladimir Putin, half-shrouded in shadow, conveying mystery and authority. Next, Susan Glasser, identified as 'The New Yorker', appears in a color interview, wearing a red blazer, speaking with a composed, analytical demeanor. She asserts that "They let Vladimir Putin arguably get away with it in Georgia," elaborating that "One message that Putin clearly takes from that in hindsight is that he is able to uh use the military as a tool of uh achieving his political ends outside the borders of Russia, and that the West will complain and lecture, but basically will let him get away with it." Glasser concludes this is "a terrible message and I think it is the prologue for the Ukraine disaster." The sequence continues with a black-and-white profile shot of a thoughtful man, likely a political figure, before concluding with a green-tinted satellite view of The White House, overlaid with digital targeting graphics and a 'SIGNAL STATUS' bar, creating a tense, high-stakes atmosphere as the ominous music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a digital satellite interface displaying Earth from space, green targeting graphics zooming into Moscow, Russia, indicating a strategic focus, accompanied by news snippets announcing Barack Obama as the 'First African-American president of the United States' and his impending visit to Russia. A narrator, later identified as William Burns, U.S. Ambassador to Russia (2005–08), states that 'less than a year later, Vladimir Putin would meet newly elected Barack Obama face-to-face,' with news voices adding that Obama has 'a big meeting ahead,' 'sizing up his third American president.' Burns continues, noting 'the shadows of the Cold War will loom over his summit.' The scene transitions to a bustling Moscow street where a black presidential limousine, adorned with American and Russian flags, drives through traffic. Obama exits the vehicle, greeted by a man in a suit, and proceeds into a grand, ornate room with classical decor. He shakes hands with Vladimir Putin, both in dark suits, and they sit across from each other for a formal meeting, with aides and notepads nearby. Burns, speaking from a dimly lit room, recalls their 'first meeting in July of 2009 at Putin's dacha, just outside Moscow,' describing them as 'much different personalities' and recounting how Obama's 'initial question, about 10 seconds, led to a 45-minute monologue by Putin.' Antony Blinken, V.P. Biden’s National Security Adviser (2009–13), also speaking from a dimly lit room, provides commentary on Putin's communication style, stating he is 'extraordinarily calm, matter-of-fact,' doesn't 'get ruffled,' and 'may say hard things, but he says them in a very calm, almost matter-of-fact way.' Blinken further characterizes Putin as someone who 'simply speaks mis-truth after mis-truth and tries to misinform, but he does it as easily as he breathes.' The video returns to Obama and Putin seated in the ornate meeting room, engaged in conversation, with Putin gesturing as he speaks, while aides observe and take notes, all set against a backdrop of dramatic, underlying music, emphasizing the formal and tense atmosphere of high-stakes diplomacy.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Antony Blinken, a man with graying hair in a dark suit and blue tie, speaking seriously in a dimly lit room with bookshelves, his clear, serious voice recounting how Obama would tell Putin, "we're not blind, we have eyes, we can see," only for Putin to move on. A male narrator then explains that inside the administration, Vice President Biden urged a tough response, as black-and-white archival footage shows Joe Biden, in a dark suit and striped tie, placing a hand on Barack Obama’s shoulder while conversing near a snow-covered window, followed by a focus on Biden’s intense expression. Tomichah Tillemann, identified as 'Sen. Biden adviser, 2005–09', then speaks in a modern interview setting, his analytical voice stating that Joe Biden knew Putin was acting like a playground bully and would continue to do so. The scene shifts to black-and-white footage of a man in a white shirt and patterned tie gesturing, as the narrator introduces CIA Director John Brennan, whose voice then explains that bullies need to get their "nose bloodied." Joe Biden, now in a blue suit and purple tie, is seen speaking with a serious demeanor in a dimly lit room. The narrator continues, noting that officials at the Pentagon and State Department joined with Biden, advocating arming Ukraine with Javelin anti-tank missiles, as black-and-white footage shows Barack Obama seated at a long conference table with officials, including a military officer, and Biden standing with a stern expression. Susan Glasser, identified as 'The New Yorker', then speaks directly to the camera, her analytical voice emphasizing that "they're all saying, 'You should give military assistance to Ukraine... Putin is getting the wrong message from us,' and Obama wouldn't do it." The segment concludes with a somber background score and a black-and-white image of Barack Obama covering his face in distress, while Biden sits beside him with clasped hands, underscoring a moment of emotional weight or crisis.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white archival shot of Barack Obama, visibly distressed and covering his face with his hands, while Joe Biden sits beside him, clasping his hands in a contemplative gesture. A male voice narrates, stating that "Biden lost" and "instead of weapons, Obama went with sanctions," all set against a somber, low-key instrumental score. The scene transitions to a close-up of Joe Biden, his expression somber and reflective, as Evan Osnos's voice begins to explain Obama's perspective on not being elected to focus on Russia and Ukraine. The video then cuts to a color interview with Evan Osnos, identified by on-screen text as 'Evan Osnos, The New Yorker,' speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room, continuing his analysis about the Vice President's push to "draw a bright line" and declare that "Russian aggression cannot stand." This is followed by a series of intense black-and-white close-ups of Vladimir Putin, his face dramatically illuminated from the side, conveying a stern and penetrating gaze, as Osnos's voice continues, concluding that Obama's internal sentiment was, "Sorry, this is this is just not a top priority for us." The video returns to Putin’s face, now in an extreme close-up, his eyes fixed and unblinking, creating a sense of psychological intensity. The sequence then cuts to Timothy Snyder, identified as 'Author, The Road to Unfreedom,' speaking in a color interview with an earnest and animated expression, explaining that "Putin takes a lesson from Ukraine" when he can "invade a country" with "minimal" reaction. The final frames return to Putin’s eyes, slowly zooming in, heightening the dramatic and confrontational atmosphere, as Snyder concludes his analysis, warning that Putin learns "this kind of thing can work if we gen it up properly," a message not intended to be sent. The somber instrumental score fades out.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sweeping aerial time-lapse of Washington, D.C., transitioning from late afternoon to dusk, showcasing the city’s iconic skyline including the Washington Monument and the U.S. Capitol, with traffic flowing along highways and the Potomac River reflecting the fading light. Upbeat, slightly tense background music begins as a female news anchor states, "The 2016 presidential race is underway." A male narrator adds, "Having tested the United States' resolve, Putin would go even further." The scene shifts to a ground-level view of the Washington Monument at sunset, framed by bare tree branches, followed by a dramatic twilight shot of the Capitol Building as the sky turns deep purple and pink, with its dome illuminated. The news anchor continues, "And the candidates are once again fanned out across..." and the narrator interjects, "Striking at the heart of American democracy." The anchor mentions, "Candidates make a final push to shore up..." before the narrator concludes, "Interfering in the 2016 presidential election," with the anchor adding, "Just a few days away." The sequence then cuts to an interview with Fiona Hill, identified as 'Nat'l. Security Council, 2017–19,' wearing a red collared shirt and speaking expressively in a dimly lit indoor setting. She explains in a clear, articulate voice with a slight accent, "There's two goals there: discredit our election, and also to discredit the presidency, to neutralize the United States as an adversary, to really tarnish US leadership as, you know, the leader of the democratic world, the free world, the West." The FRONTLINE logo is visible. This is followed by a night-time time-lapse of the city’s highways, with light trails from vehicles creating dynamic streaks, accompanied by the continuing background music. The video then transitions to a close-up of blinking yellow lights on server hardware, suggesting digital infrastructure, before showing a screen of WikiLeaks with the text 'Search the DNC email data' and multiple overlapping email documents. The male narrator's voice returns, stating, "Putin's attack was aimed at Washington D.C. Russian hackers breaching Democratic Party computer servers. Email leaks designed to undermine Hillary Clinton's presidential campaign." The final segment features an interview with James Clapper, identified as 'Dir. of National Intelligence, 2010–17,' wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with serious, deliberate hand gestures in a dimly lit room. He asserts in a deep, serious voice, "This was the most aggressive, most direct, and most assertive campaign that the Russians ever mounted in the history of our elections. And..." The FRONTLINE logo appears in the bottom left corner during both interviews.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of server hardware, its blinking green lights and tangled cables suggesting a digital or cyber context, while a male voice (later identified as Stephen Hadley) discusses the "variety and intensity of the techniques that they employed." This transitions into a rapid montage of social media posts and news headlines, many referencing 'DCLeaks,' Hillary Clinton, and conspiracy theories, including claims about FBI agents, WikiLeaks, and political scandals, with images of Hillary Clinton and Bill Clinton, tweets, and sensationalized articles. A narrator's voice, accompanied by a low, suspenseful hum, details "misinformation," "fake users designed to look like Americans," "trolls supporting the Kremlin," "an army of automated bots on Twitter," and "targeted advertisements on Facebook and Google," all "calculated to hurt Clinton and help her rival, Donald Trump." The scene shifts to black-and-white footage of Hillary Clinton speaking at a podium, followed by Donald Trump standing solemnly beside her, both in front of a backdrop of the U.S. Constitution. An interview with Stephen Hadley, identified as National Security Adviser 2005–09, is then shown in a dimly lit room with a 'FRONTLINE' logo, where he explains "President Putin's view of Trump was a pension for authoritarianism, backing off of human rights, and talking about wanting to have a more positive relationship with Russia," asking, "What's not to like if you're Vladimir Putin?" and concluding, "he's one of us." This is followed by a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, his face dramatically lit, conveying intensity. The scene changes to a bright, colorful daytime shot of the White House, with a fountain and vibrant red tulips in the foreground. As news anchors announce "Donald Trump pulled off one of the biggest political upsets in America," and declare "a victory for Trump and for Putin," stating "Donald Trump is elected the 45th President," and "shocking the nation and the world," the final shot shows Donald Trump speaking at a podium with the presidential seal, an American flag behind him, and a golden curtain backdrop, gesturing emphatically.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, speaking emphatically at a podium with an American flag and golden curtains behind him, his voice heard stating he was congratulated on winning the election. The scene then cuts to Peter Baker, co-author of 'The Divider,' speaking in a dimly lit room, explaining that from Putin's perspective, the favored candidate won and disrupted America's faith in democracy. As Baker speaks, the visuals shift to an urban protest with demonstrators holding 'FIGHT TRUMP' and 'AGAINST HATE' signs, accompanied by audible crowd noise and sirens. This transitions to a chaotic street scene featuring a burning vehicle and smoke, while Baker's voice concludes that America is too distracted to pose a threat to Putin. A dramatic, suspenseful musical score begins as a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, dramatically lit, appears. The scene then shows a green-tinted satellite view of The White House, overlaid with targeting graphics, before zooming into a similar view of The Kremlin, as a female news anchor mentions 'President Trump's' and 'President Vladimir Putin.' Trump and Putin are then seen walking side-by-side on a red carpet, followed by Melania Trump, and posing for a photo, while a male narrator states that Vladimir Putin would confront a new American president for the fourth time, and another male news anchor urges confronting Putin about Russia's 2016 election interference. The video cuts to John Bolton, National Security Adviser 2018–19, speaking in an interview, his voice describing Vladimir Putin as 'clear-eyed and cold-blooded' and relentlessly pursuing Russia's national interest. The segment concludes with Trump and Putin seated for a diplomatic meeting in an ornate room with American and Russian flags, surrounded by aides and photographers, including a camera operator in the foreground, as Bolton's voice fades and the dramatic music continues.

The narrative shifts to a protest in an urban setting, with demonstrators holding signs such as 'FIGHT TRUMP' and 'AGAINST HATE,' one featuring a crossed-out swastika. The video cuts to John Bolton, identified as National Security Adviser 2018–19, speaking in an interview. The final scene shows Trump and Putin seated for a diplomatic meeting in an ornate room with American and Russian flags, surrounded by aides and photographers, with a camera operator in the foreground capturing the event.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with close-ups of Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, and Vladimir Putin, in a dark suit and patterned tie, both seated before American and Russian flags. A female voice, identified as Yamiche Alcindor, describes Trump as sitting "up in his chair looking as though he really, really wants to get something out of this meeting." The scene transitions to a medium shot of the leaders in an ornate room with flags and white flowers, where Trump gestures as he speaks. Trump's distinct voice is heard congratulating Putin, saying, "Well, first of all, Mr. President, I'd like to congratulate you on a really great World Cup." A subtle "whoosh" sound and background chatter are present during this exchange. Alcindor's voice then notes Putin is "kind of slumped in his chair, not smiling, doesn't look like he's having a good time," as Trump's voice continues, asserting that "getting along with Russia is a good thing, not a bad thing." The video briefly shows Trump and Putin shaking hands before returning to their seated positions. An interview with John Bolton, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, follows, where he speaks with expressive hand gestures. Bolton's voice provides analysis, stating, "It was clear that in Trump, Putin had a very different American president," and that "Trump never understood... what the nature of Putin's game was." He suggests Putin "thought Trump was a fool and easily manipulable." The segment concludes with Putin seated alone in the ornate meeting room as aides pass by, and Bolton's voice remarks, "The cameras were sent away."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white scene of Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin seated across from each other in a formal meeting room, flanked by American and Russian flags, with a small table holding white flowers and papers between them. A close-up on Putin reveals his neutral, contemplative expression, as a male narrator with a deep voice explains, accompanied by ominous, low background music, that "For two hours, they met privately, without any aides." The narrator continues, stating that "Putin didn't need the KGB's psychological profilers to hand him a dossier on how to play Donald Trump," and that "He was able to read Trump... He knew how to play Trump like a violin." The scene transitions to a color interview with Eugene Robinson, identified by on-screen text, a man with glasses and a mustache wearing a dark blazer over a blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera. The video then cuts back to black-and-white footage of Trump and Putin shaking hands, followed by a color shot of them walking together through a grand hallway, surrounded by aides. The setting shifts to a large, opulent hall with chandeliers and flags, where they stand at podiums labeled 'HELSINKI 2018' during a joint press conference, facing a seated audience of journalists and officials. The narrator states, "Afterwards, when they faced the press, a shocking statement." A reporter in a dark suit raises a microphone, asking in a clear voice, "President Putin denied having anything to do with the election interference in 2016. Every US intelligence agency has concluded that Russia did. My first question for you, sir, is, who do you believe?" Another voice echoes, "Who do you believe?" Eugene Robinson then comments, "That's the starkest possible way to put that question to the president." Trump is shown speaking at the Helsinki 2018 podium, with American and Russian flags behind him, maintaining a serious demeanor, his distinct voice heard stating, "My people came to me, Dan Coats came to me and some others, they said, they think it's Russia. Uh, I have uh President..." The segment includes an interview with Greg Miller of The Washington Post, identified by on-screen text, speaking in a dimly lit setting, before concluding with Trump continuing his speech at the Helsinki 2018 podium.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, speaking at a podium labeled 'HELSINKI 2018', flanked by American and Russian flags. His voice is heard stating, "Putin, he just said it's not Russia. I will say this, I don't see any reason why it would be." The camera then cuts to a wider shot showing both Trump and Vladimir Putin at separate podiums, Putin calmly dressed in a dark suit. The scene alternates between close-ups of Trump speaking, where he is heard describing President Putin as "extremely strong and powerful in his denial today," and Putin listening or speaking with a composed expression. An audience of reporters and officials is visible in the foreground, with a male commentator's voice expressing disbelief, "I felt frozen to my chair like everybody else in the room, we couldn't believe it." A female commentator's voice describes how "everybody who was in the room with them just saw how masterfully Putin manipulated Trump," further adding that Trump was seen as "gullible and stupid and easily manipulatable," and that Putin was "just making ropes out of him, just braiding him like a braid." After the speeches, Trump and Putin shake hands and walk off stage. The scene transitions to a digital satellite interface with green targeting graphics, zooming from Earth to 'THE WHITE HOUSE, WASHINGTON, D.C.', as a male commentator calls it an "extraordinary moment in American history" and a "disgraceful play by the President of the United States as he stood by Vladimir Putin." The satellite view gives way to a blurred, fast-moving shot of the White House grounds with American flags waving, ending in a chaotic park view, while commentators discuss how "concern continues to grow about what they said to each other behind closed doors," even calling it a "bromance."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a blurred, fast-moving shot of a park-like setting, possibly near the White House, featuring a fountain and trees, set to a serious, investigative background music. Susan Glasser, identified as 'Co-author, The Divider,' appears in a dimly lit interview, wearing a red blazer and patterned scarf, her voice heard discussing a "Trump romance" with Putin and how "Trump acted in some ways as an arm of Putin's foreign policy." Multiple male narrators then describe "President Trump on the attack, asking what good is NATO," and "questioning the entire point to one of America's oldest alliances," further stating that "Trump was echoing Putin's talking points on the key battleground of Ukraine," and that "Ukraine is one of the most corrupt countries in the world." Glasser's voice reinforces this, saying, "He would say Ukraine is a really corrupt country." The visual transitions to dynamic, handheld clips of Donald Trump interacting with reporters outdoors, surrounded by microphones and cameras, gesturing emphatically and speaking animatedly in a dark suit with either a red or blue tie. Close-ups of his hands and face are shown, along with wider shots of him walking toward a helicopter on the White House lawn. During these visuals, a female narrator states, "The President is saying that Crimea probably should belong to Russia because everybody there speaks Russian," a point Glasser's voice reiterates, "Crimea is Russian. Everybody there speaks Russian and wants to be part of Russia." A male narrator then explicitly states, "Donald Trump parroting Vladimir Putin, saying that Ukraine is not even a real country." Glasser's voice questions, "Where did he hear these things? He heard them from Putin," and adds, "You know, and he would just echo them." Another male narrator concludes that "Trump is actually helping Putin reconstitute the Soviet Union," to which Glasser's voice responds, "It was just too easy." The segment concludes with a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, seated in an ornate chair with a serious, slightly smirking expression, as the narrator's voice is cut off mid-sentence.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a series of black-and-white close-ups of Vladimir Putin, seated in an ornate chair, his expression shifting from serious and slightly smirking to contemplative, then an intense stare. Over these visuals, a male voiceover discusses the pointlessness of continuing to punish Russia. This transitions to color interview footage of Marie Yovanovitch, identified by on-screen text as 'U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine, 2016–19,' speaking earnestly in a dimly lit room, wearing glasses, a dark blazer, and a gold necklace, gesturing with her hands. Her voice is heard stating, "He's getting pretty much everything he needs from President Trump," detailing how Putin is "getting the smackdown of NATO, the humiliation of our best allies," and that "the list goes on and on and on." Yovanovitch continues, explaining that "Putin was probably betting on a second term of President Trump and hoping that he could get his way on many things, whether it's NATO, whether it's Ukraine, without using the instrument of war." The scene then shifts to a Fox News studio with two anchors at a desk. A male voiceover states, "But Trump did not get a second term." The Fox News anchors, a man and a woman, are seen as the male anchor announces, "The Fox News decision desk can now project that former Vice President Joe Biden..." A glitchy, colorful transition then displays an NBC News graphic declaring 'PRESIDENT ELECT JOE BIDEN.' Another male voiceover adds, "And with a new president..." followed by the female Fox News anchor completing the projection, "elect Joseph Robinette Biden." Donald Trump is then shown speaking at a podium with American flags behind him, his voice heard declaring, "Frankly, we did win this election!" accompanied by crowd cheers and applause. A male voiceover describes "an escalation of the chaos in America that Putin helped foment." The video then shows a chaotic street scene with a black presidential limousine, adorned with American and Russian flags, surrounded by a crowd. A voiceover mentions, "The president showing no signs that he's prepared to concede this election..." followed by another voice stating, "Division. This is a contested election. Many people are nervous..." and finally, "Violence." The segment concludes with a large protest march toward the U.S. Capitol, with demonstrators holding signs like 'COUNT EVERY LEGAL VOTE' and 'TRUMP 2020,' under a clear sky, as sounds of a crowd and general commotion continue.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with chaotic, handheld footage at night, showing police in riot gear arresting protesters near the U.S. Capitol amidst sounds of struggle, shouting, and sirens, as a voice declares "insurrection." The scene quickly shifts to daytime, wide-angle shots of the U.S. Capitol Building under siege by a massive crowd of protesters, many wearing red hats and waving 'TRUMP 2020' flags, breaching the exterior and climbing its steps and walls, while inside, chaos continues as protesters surge through arched doorways. Over these visuals, Timothy Snyder, identified as 'Author, The Road to Unfreedom,' states that "Nobody loved January 6th more than the Russians did," explaining that "Trump gave them four years, which was one big gift. But January 6th was like the wrapping, the beautiful wrapping, you know, the package. January 6th, apart from anything else, leads directly to the war in Ukraine. Because it looks like America is not just morally discredited, looks like America is weak." This is followed by bodycam footage from January 6, 2021, showing police in riot gear being overwhelmed by protesters, with one officer pushed to the ground, accompanied by continued sounds of struggle. The video then transitions to a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin with a stern expression, followed by a green-tinted satellite view interface zooming from the White House to Geneva, Switzerland, with targeting graphics. A news report voiceover announces a "historic meeting with Russian President Vladimir... soon in Geneva," where "Vladimir Putin faced his old nemesis, President Biden," noting that "for neither of them is this their first rodeo. The tension seemed to be a bit high." The scene shifts to June 2021, showing Joe Biden and Vladimir Putin shaking hands on a red carpet in Geneva, flanked by U.S. and Russian flags, with Biden placing a hand on Putin’s arm as they walk into a building. His people are so divided.'" The final shot returns to Biden and Putin shaking hands, with Biden smiling and Putin maintaining a neutral expression, as they enter the building together, as Osnos's commentary concludes.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Joe Biden, in a dark suit and blue tie, shaking hands with Vladimir Putin, in a dark suit and patterned purple tie, as they enter a grand library flanked by American and Russian flags. They then sit across from each other in ornate wooden chairs, a floral arrangement and large globe on the table between them, against a backdrop of tall bookshelves. A male narrator's voice immediately sets a strategic tone, noting the need to 'hold Biden off long enough so that he can continue to assemble the plan that he's putting together.' The camera alternates between close-ups of their serious expressions—Biden occasionally adjusting, Putin maintaining a composed, steady gaze—as the narrator describes them as 'children of the Cold War,' adding that 'Biden saw Putin for what he is, a KGB revanchist who wants to remake the Russian Empire.' A brief black-and-white interlude shows a man writing notes. The scene returns to color, now including Antony Blinken, identified by another narrator as 'now Secretary of State,' taking notes to Biden’s left, and another aide to Putin’s right. The first narrator describes their 'body language' as 'icy,' emphasizing that 'these are two people who do not like each other.' The second narrator concludes by stating the conversation's tone was 'professional' and 'direct,' but 'President Biden made very clear where we saw things differently, and so did President Putin,' underscoring the clear disagreements despite the formal setting. The warm, consistent lighting emphasizes the gravity of this tense, high-stakes diplomatic encounter.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a formal diplomatic meeting between Joe Biden and Vladimir Putin, seated across from each other in an ornate library setting, flanked by the U.S. and Russian flags, with Antony Blinken taking notes. A male narrator states that a "top priority for Biden" was "deterring Putin in Ukraine," followed by another male voice explaining that "President Biden made clear in the meeting to President Putin, our commitment to Ukraine, our commitment to its sovereignty, to its independence, to its territorial integrity." The scene transitions to an exterior shot of a grand building with a red carpet and flags, where Putin and his entourage exit and approach a black limousine. Julia Ioffe, a woman with shoulder-length brown hair, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room, is heard explaining that "they wanted to park Putin but he didn't want to be parked," and that "he wanted to be the number one problem of everybody in the whole world," especially "the number one priority, the number one threat, the number one problem that the White House was dealing with." The footage then cuts to a Russian presidential aircraft, marked 'РОССИЯ', taking off from a runway. This is followed by a series of military scenes: soldiers in white winter uniforms saluting in front of a hangar filled with armored vehicles, and tanks moving through snowy fields and along a snow-covered road, all accompanied by intense, dramatic music and Russian military commands like "Броня!" and "Старт!". The narrator returns, stating that "Putin began laying the groundwork for the invasion of Ukraine," with another male voice adding that "100,000 Russian troops" were near Ukraine's border, "fueling fears" as "Russian forces start moving back toward Ukraine's borders." The segment concludes with William Taylor, identified as U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine (2006–09), speaking in an interview setting with the FRONTLINE logo visible, his voice describing "heavy military equipment clearly taking an aggressive posture," not just at the "eastern border."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a green-tinted satellite view interface, displaying a map of Eastern Europe with Ukraine highlighted, bordered by Belarus, Russia, and Crimea, while a male narrator describes "Russian troops surrounding Ukraine for days now," specifically mentioning the "Ukraine border with Belarus," "Ukraine border with Russia," and "Ukraine border with occupied Crimea." Another narrator adds that "satellite images show an increase in Russian forces on the Ukraine border," as a 'SIGNAL STATUS' bar is visible. The camera zooms into snowy military bases in Russia and Crimea, under a digital overlay, reinforcing the visual of troop movements. The scene transitions to a black-and-white meeting room where President Joe Biden, seated at the head of a long table with the Presidential Seal and American flags, listens intently with his hand on his chin. A narrator states that "an American president was facing a critical decision about how to deal with Putin." Antony Blinken, identified as 'Secretary of State' in a color interview, speaks with a serious tone, explaining that "it was in that moment, when a combination of seeing the forces deployed and then having from the information we'd gotten, real visibility on what Russia was actually thinking and what they were planning. That's when we really saw the storm rising and heading in Ukraine's direction." Finally, Marie Yovanovitch, identified as 'U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine, 2016–19,' speaks in a dimly lit room, gesturing as she warns, "If Russia is allowed to get away with this, if Russia stomps all over Ukraine and takes it over." Throughout, a serious, suspenseful orchestral background music underscores the geopolitical tension.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Marie Yovanovitch, identified as 'U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine, 2016–19,' speaking in a dimly lit color interview, wearing glasses and a dark blazer, her clear voice stating that the situation "affects the entire international order that we've been living under since World War II" and that "the stakes were pretty high and they continued to be very high." The scene transitions to black-and-white archival footage of Joe Biden, appearing contemplative at a long table with American flags, then in profile during a video conference with Vladimir Putin, whose image is on a large screen in a dark, formal room with Russian flags. A male narrator explains that "In a video conference with Putin, Biden warned him to pull back from the brink." Antony Blinken's voice then describes Biden's message, saying, "He's saying first of all, we see what you're doing, what you're planning, what you're plotting. And if you go ahead with what we're seeing, you need to understand the costs and consequences to Russia will be severe, and you need to know that, um, I mean what I say." The video cuts to a color interview with Antony Blinken, 'Secretary of State,' speaking directly to the camera with an American flag behind him. The narrator then states, "Putin's response was familiar. Denial," as dramatic, low-frequency music plays. The scene alternates between Blinken's interview and black-and-white close-ups of Putin, who gestures intensely. Blinken continues, explaining, "They're denying, uh, they're denying any imminent aggression against Ukraine. They're saying that that's not what we're doing." He concludes by stating, "But because we had such extraordinary visibility and extraordinary information, we knew in real time when Putin was saying something that was in contradiction with what was actually happening," as the final shot shows a serious, pensive Putin looking off to the side.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, his expression stern and contemplative, hands clasped against a dark background with a faint Russian flag motif, as a somber, dramatic score begins. A male narrator states that "30 years after the fall of the Soviet Union, Vladimir Putin was now pursuing an all-out war to rebuild the Russian Empire." The scene transitions to a nighttime Ukrainian cityscape with illuminated buildings, then cuts to a dramatic orange glow on the horizon, suggesting an explosion or fire, accompanied by a siren. On-screen text reads 'I made a decision to carry out a special military operation,' which Putin himself declares in Russian, followed by sounds of explosions and distant gunfire. Dark, smoky silhouettes of explosions and soldiers appear in the distance, then a missile streaks across a daytime city sky, followed by a building engulfed in flames and smoke. An aerial shot reveals a heavily damaged urban area with collapsed buildings and rubble, all underscored by continuous explosions, gunfire, and shellfire. A narrator describes "apocalyptic scenes on the outskirts of the capital city" and a "non-stop onslaught over the last 24 hours with mass casualties," with "tens of thousands of civilians trapped in cities under near constant Russian shellfire." A black-and-white profile of Putin reappears, his face intensely illuminated from the side, before cutting to Julia Ioffe, identified as 'Founding partner, Puck Media,' speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room. Her clear female voice explains, "Putin wants Ukraine. He wants this pan-Slavic superstate with Moscow as the capital... He doesn't think it's a real country. I don't know how you compromise with somebody who just wants Ukraine. That's not something you can build a bridge across." The video then shows chaotic, shaky footage of a war-torn environment: a burning vehicle, a man running through debris, and a soldier in camouflage moving through a destroyed urban area with smoke and fire. Another narrator states, "Ukraine is calling this a full-scale invasion," as the sounds of explosions and chaos intensify. The final frames depict a man carrying a child through a damaged street, emphasizing the human toll of the conflict, with the narrator concluding, "biggest war..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with chaotic, shaky footage of a war-torn urban environment, showing damaged buildings, debris, and a blackened, smoke-filled sky, suggesting recent bombardment, as a male voice (Joe Biden) states, "Europe since World War II." The camera pans across a desolate street with abandoned vehicles and a damaged van, emphasizing destruction, while a female voice (Susan Glasser) describes, "The world is waiting to see just how far Putin's 190,000 troops will go into the country of Ukraine." The scene shifts to a dimly lit interior where civilians, including a woman in a floral dress and a child, lie huddled on the floor, appearing traumatized and exhausted, with a wheelchair nearby, indicating displacement and suffering, underscored by the poignant sound of a child crying. Joe Biden's voice then asks, "Will he stop?" before a male narrator declares, "With the battle for Ukraine underway." The tone abruptly changes to a stark black-and-white office setting, where Joe Biden, a man with white hair in a dark suit and striped tie, is seated at a large ornate desk, writing intently, with an American flag and presidential seal visible. He then speaks with expressive hand gestures, flanked by an aide, as the narrator explains how "Biden went further than any president before, delivering weapons and intelligence to the front lines to help fight Putin." The scene cuts to Susan Glasser, a woman with short brown hair in a red blazer and patterned scarf, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting. The video then transitions to aerial combat footage: a snowy landscape with military vehicles, followed by a massive explosion on a road with tanks and armored vehicles, and then a wide shot of a field with multiple explosions and smoke trails. During this, Kori Schake, a woman with shoulder-length wavy brown hair, wearing a navy blazer with red lapels and a yellow-and-blue ribbon, speaks in a color interview, identified by on-screen text, stating, "The United States has opted in to the fight. The amount and scale of the military support for Ukraine's defense... is enormous. It's staggering, clearly something that Vladimir Putin did not expect." A close-up black-and-white profile of Vladimir Putin, intense and focused, appears as the narrator describes "The US and NATO allies are working quickly to arm Ukraine," and that "Russian forces caught off guard, apparently in disarray." The narrator continues, "Russia thought the invasion would be over swiftly. That was before their jets were plummeting from Ukrainian skies." The segment ends with a return to the war-torn urban environment, showing a shattered glass view with a blurred vehicle behind, reinforcing the theme of destruction and vulnerability, while Susan Glasser's voice concludes, "He clearly miscalculated the capabilities of the Russian military."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Kori Schake, Director of the American Enterprise Institute, speaking in a close-up interview shot, wearing a navy blazer with red lapels and a yellow-and-blue ribbon supporting Ukraine. Her clear, articulate voice discusses the Ukrainian military's resolve and describes the conflict as a "miscalculation of Napoleonic magnitude." The scene abruptly shifts to a violent explosion in a green, wooded area, followed by a fiery blast engulfing a structure, suggesting a war zone, as a male news anchor declares, "Ukraine's president remains defiant." The anchor continues, noting "Ukraine's resolve has shocked the world and frustrated the Russian" forces, as the footage transitions to a serene view of the Kremlin, then a black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin with a stern expression. Another male voice reports that "Putin is facing the embarrassment of this offensive not necessarily being pulled off as smoothly as he" expected, as a montage of news clips begins. This is heard over a rural Ukrainian village under attack, protesters being arrested, and Putin seated at a desk, with news banners like 'CBS Mornings | BREAKING OVERNIGHT' and 'ABC News | PUTIN UNDER PRESSURE' reporting that "even as his army was pushed back, Putin was doubling down." A CNBC news anchor reports, "Coming out of Russia, where Vladimir Putin is preparing for a longer and more intense war on Ukraine," as Putin is shown on CNBC with the headline 'EUROPE FACES ENERGY CRISIS AS PUTIN THREATENS TOTAL ENERGY CUT-OFF', detailing that "President Putin suddenly announcing he would call up 300,000 men for military service." A glitchy transition to Fox News shows soldiers in camouflage as an anchor states, "Waging economic warfare," and quotes Putin: "No gas, no oil, nothing, he said, over a long winter." The segment concludes with Putin again on Fox News, reinforcing the nuclear threat, as the anchor warns of "raising the specter of nuclear annihilation," and "now threatening to use nuclear weapons," with a prominent 'PUTIN THREATENING TO USE NUCLEAR WEAPONS' banner.
segments.visual_original_caption: The segment opens with Kori Schake, Director of the American Enterprise Institute, speaking in a close-up interview shot; she wears a navy blazer with red lapels and a yellow-and-blue ribbon, indicating support for Ukraine. The scene abruptly shifts to a violent explosion in a green, wooded area, followed by a fiery blast engulfing a structure, suggesting a war zone. The footage then transitions to a serene view of the Kremlin, with its golden domes and Russian flag waving under a partly cloudy sky, contrasting the earlier chaos. A black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin follows, showing him in a suit with a stern expression. The video then cuts to a montage of news clips: a rural Ukrainian village under attack with smoke rising, labeled 'CBS Mornings | BREAKING OVERNIGHT'; protesters being arrested in a city square, with a sign reading 'ЧЕСТНЫЕ ВЫБОРЫ' (Fair Elections); and Putin seated at a desk, with ABC News reporting 'PUTIN UNDER PRESSURE' after calling up more troops. The montage continues with Putin on CNBC, with the headline 'EUROPE FACES ENERGY CRISIS AS PUTIN THREATENS TOTAL ENERGY CUT-OFF,' followed by a glitchy transition to Fox News, which declares 'PUTIN THREATENING TO USE NUCLEAR WEAPONS' as soldiers in camouflage are shown in a field. The segment ends with Putin again on Fox News, reinforcing the nuclear threat, with the same breaking news banner. The sequence juxtaposes expert commentary with real-time war footage and media coverage, emphasizing escalating geopolitical tension and the human cost of conflict.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a Fox News breaking news segment, displaying Vladimir Putin in a formal setting with the Russian flag behind him, as red banners declare 'PUTIN THREATENS TO USE NUCLEAR WEAPONS' and 'FOX NEWS ALERT'. A male voice briefly states, "I'm not bluffing." The scene quickly transitions to Antony Blinken, Secretary of State, in a color interview, wearing a dark suit with a U.S. flag pin, speaking seriously with hand gestures against an American flag backdrop. Blinken, with a calm and analytical male voice, expresses deep concern over "Vladimir Putin doubling and tripling down on the aggression" through "mobilization," "the purported annexation of Ukrainian territory," and "loose talk about nuclear weapons," predicting this "is likely to continue for some time." The video then cuts to a stark black-and-white close-up of Vladimir Putin, his face dramatically illuminated, emphasizing his intense, focused gaze. This is followed by a similar black-and-white close-up of another man with a stern expression, likely a U.S. official. During these monochrome visuals, a deep, dramatic male narrator describes "Vladimir Putin, the former KGB agent, hell-bent on rebuilding the Russian Empire," now "a dangerous pariah on the world stage," "staring down yet another American president to see how far he will go." The sequence then shifts to a man in a suit and glasses, speaking directly to the camera in a color interview, his expression serious and engaged. With a clear, articulate male voice, he analyzes that "Putin is escalating into a war with the West more broadly," by "cutting off energy, threatening nuclear weapons," and that "part of the reason is to undercut Western resolve and to make the West eager to get this over with." The entire segment uses dramatic lighting and tight framing, alternating between color and monochrome, to heighten the tension and convey the urgency and gravity of the geopolitical narrative.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man, identified by multiple speakers as Vladimir Putin, shown from behind in a dark suit with a white collared shirt, his receding hairline visible as he walks forward. He is silhouetted against a bright, gradient background, while various voices discuss his actions: one male voice mentions him cutting off natural gas supply amidst a "looming energy crisis," another male voice states he's "hoping that he can put pressure on the West to back off," and two female voices discuss him "counting on the fact that the Western powers are just going to lose interest in Ukraine" and questioning "how much longer the United States can continue to help bankroll this war." A third male voice asks if "Ukraine ultimately be left to fight this war on its own." As dark curtains with ornate designs part on either side, emphasizing a formal setting, a ticking sound begins, followed by a deep, ominous musical note as the scene fades to black. This transitions into a series of funding and production credits, displayed in white text over a dark, digital-style background with faint blurred imagery and horizontal light streaks, all set to dramatic, orchestral, and suspenseful music with a strong percussive, almost militaristic beat. The credits list sponsors like the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, PBS Viewers, Abrams Foundation, MacArthur Foundation, Park Foundation, Heising-Simons Foundation, and the FRONTLINE Journalism Fund (with its red rectangular logo). Production details for 'Putin and the Presidents' are shown, crediting Michael Kirk as director and Vanessa Fica as reporter, along with archival sources. The segment concludes with a male narrator stating, "For more on this and other Frontline programs, visit our website at pbs.org/frontline," as the URL pbs.org/frontline remains visible in the lower right corner.

As he walks, the camera slowly zooms out, revealing more of his figure and the surrounding environment. On either side, dark curtains begin to part, revealing ornate, possibly heraldic, designs—suggesting a formal or ceremonial setting. The segment concludes with a URL for more information: pbs.org/frontline, displayed in the lower right corner throughout the credits.
